text: In the operating room, the surgeon performs a laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy. After lysing adhesions, the transverse colon and omentum are retracted cephalad to expose the ligament of Treitz. The dilated third portion of the duodenum is visible to the right of the middle colic vessels. A window is created in the transverse mesocolon, and blunt dissection frees the duodenum from retroperitoneal and mesocolic attachments in a Cattell-Braasch maneuver. The jejunum is drawn 40 centimeters from the ligament of Treitz and placed adjacent to the duodenum without tension. A stay suture is placed, and entotomies are created with hook electrocautery. A side-to-side anastomosis is formed using a 60mm laparoscopic linear stapling device with a 2.5mm staple load. The common enterotomy is closed with running IIO absorbable suture in a single layer.

The surgeon places a reinforcement suture at the apex of the anastomosis to minimize strain. The efferent limb of the jejunum is sutured to the retroperitoneum with permanent sutures to prevent kinking. An intraoperative endoscopy is performed to evaluate the anastomosis, and an air leak test is conducted, showing no leaks.
